meta.title: X250 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Jaguar XF X250 engine — 2.7/3.0 TDV6, 2.2 diesel, 4.2/5.0 V8 and supercharged variants. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the TDV6 vs V8 choice explained. Get free quotes from 15 vetted Jaguar specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar XF X250 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X250 engine typically costs £2,000–£10,000 supply only [EM-QUOTE], depending on engine — the 2.2 diesel sits at £2,000–£3,800, while the 5.0 Supercharged XFR engine can reach £5,500–£10,000. Fitting adds £520–£1,170. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which X250 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 (AJ‑V6D/306DT, 2009–2015) is widely regarded as the most dependable X250 engine — it delivers strong torque, refined power delivery, and has proven durability in both Jaguar and Land Rover applications. The 2.2‑litre diesel (224DT) is also a solid choice, especially with the eight‑speed ZF gearbox introduced in 2011. Both significantly outlast the 4.2 V8 and earlier 2.7 TDV6 in terms of trouble‑free mileage.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar XF X250?

acceptedAnswer.text: Crankshaft pulley failure on the 3.0 TDV6 (common, preventable), DPF and EGR clogging on the 2.2 diesel (especially urban‑use vehicles), water pump and cooling system failures on the 5.0 V8 (both NA and supercharged), injector failure on the 2.7 TDV6, and — unrelated to the engine — rear subframe corrosion on all X250s.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar XF X250 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on the engine and maintenance history. The 3.0 TDV6 and 2.2 diesel are the best bets — they have proven durability when properly serviced. The 5.0 V8 is characterful but requires vigilant cooling system maintenance; the 4.2 V8s are now ageing and rare. The 2.7 TDV6 is solid but injector wear is common at higher mileages. A well‑maintained X250 with the right engine can be a reliable executive saloon.

mainEntity.name: Which X250 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the 5.0 Supercharged (XFR/XFR‑S) carries the highest running costs and potential timing‑chain issues on earlier cars. The 2.7 TDV6 (2008–2009) is a solid engine but injector wear can be expensive. The 4.2 V8s are now rare; parts can be harder to source than the later 5.0 units. The 3.0 TDV6 is the safest all‑round choice.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my X250 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs: crankshaft pulley wobble or noise (3.0 TDV6), persistent overheating or coolant loss (5.0 V8), rough idle or black smoke (2.7 TDV6 injectors), DPF warning lights (2.2 diesel), or loss of power and rough running (any engine). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an X250 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £12,000 X250, a £3,000–£5,500 TDV6 recon is 25–45% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £5,000 example, the same recon is 60–110% — walk away. Our 60% Jaguar threshold is the line: if the lowest viable replacement cost exceeds 60% of the vehicle's current market value, evaluate scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How long do X250 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 3.0 TDV6 and 2.2 diesel routinely exceed 150,000–200,000 miles with proper servicing (regular oil changes, DPF/EGR maintenance, crankshaft pulley inspection). The 5.0 V8 can also reach high mileages with diligent cooling system maintenance. The 2.7 TDV6 is durable but injector wear often becomes significant after 120k–150k miles. The supercharged V8s (4.2 and 5.0) can be long‑lived but costs are higher.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the X250 and X260?

acceptedAnswer.text: The X250 (2008–2015, this page) is the first‑generation Jaguar XF — built on the Ford DEW98 platform, with a range of Ford‑PSA and Jaguar‑developed V6 and V8 engines, plus later four‑cylinder options. The X260 (2015–2024) is the second‑generation XF — built on Jaguar's iQ[Al] aluminium architecture, with Ingenium four‑cylinder diesels and petrols, and the supercharged 3.0 V6. Costs, reliability and engine line‑ups differ significantly — see the X260 generation page for details.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my X250 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine serial number is stamped on the block — on V6 diesels (2.7, 3.0) it's on the left‑hand side; on V8s (4.2, 5.0) it's on the front of the block near the timing cover. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 15 vetted Jaguar specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first‑generation XF — from the 2.7 and 3.0 V6 diesels to the 5.0 V8 and the supercharged XFR and XFR‑S. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for the executive saloon that redefined Jaguar. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Jaguar XF X250 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first‑generation XF (X250) specifically — from the 2008 launch to the 2015 end of production.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any X250 engine family. Ratings are derived from triangulated specialist sources (Parkers, Autocar, PistonHeads, Jaguar forums, ADAC). The 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 and 2.2 diesel are widely regarded as the most dependable of the X250 line‑up; the 4.2 and 5.0 V8s are characterful but carry higher maintenance costs. The 2.0 turbo petrol (204PT) is a solid modern four‑cylinder option introduced with the 2012 facelift.

overview.intro: The X250 is the first‑generation Jaguar XF, launched in March 2008 as the successor to the S‑Type. Designed by Ian Callum, it introduced a bold new design language that abandoned the retro styling of its predecessor in favour of a modern, sporting aesthetic. Built on the Ford DEW98 platform — shared with the Lincoln LS and Ford Thunderbird — the X250 offered a range of Ford‑PSA and Jaguar‑developed engines: V6 and V8 diesels, naturally aspirated and supercharged V8 petrols, and later four‑cylinder diesel and petrol options. A 2012 facelift brought revised styling, new engines and an eight‑speed automatic transmission. Production ran from 2008 to 2015, with the Sportbrake estate joining the range in 2012.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Ford DEW98 (shared with Lincoln LS, Ford Thunderbird) • Predecessor: Jaguar S‑Type (X200) • Successor: Jaguar XF X260 • Sibling body styles in this generation: Saloon, Sportbrake estate.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The X250 generates consistent UK enquiries [PATTERN DATA], concentrated on 3.0 TDV6 diesel and 2.2 diesel variants as these executive saloons enter affordable‑maintenance territory.

items.quote: Most dependable: The 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 (AJ‑V6D/306DT) — a Ford‑PSA joint‑venture engine also used in Land Rover products — offers strong torque, refined power delivery, and proven durability. The 2.2‑litre diesel (224DT) is also a solid choice, especially with the later eight‑speed ZF gearbox. The 5.0 V8 supercharged (AJ133S) is the performance pick — 510 PS in XFR guise — but carries higher running costs and potential timing‑chain concerns on earlier cars. The 4.2 V8s are now ageing and less common; the naturally aspirated 3.0 V6 petrol was underpowered for the XF's weight. Buyers should prioritise a well‑maintained 3.0 TDV6 or 2.2 diesel with documented service history.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Solid Early Diesel — replaced by 3.0 for good reason
Best Diesel — the X250's most complete engine
Efficient Choice — cheap to run, reliable
Smooth V8 — cooling is the main concern
Most Expensive — XFR power comes at a cost

economicsRule.title: The X250 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000–£5,500 TDV6 recon against a £4,000–£15,000 X250 sits at 20–140% of the car's value. On a sub‑£6,000 X250, that brushes our 60% Jaguar threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £12,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 25–45% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

problems.title: 3.0 TDV6 Crankshaft Pulley Failure
problems.rootCause: The crankshaft pulley — a known weak point on the Ford‑PSA 3.0 V6 diesel — can delaminate or fail, causing loss of auxiliary drive, charging issues, and potential engine damage if the belt enters the timing case.
problems.recommendation: On any 3.0 TDV6, budget for crankshaft pulley inspection at 80k miles — a £200–£500 preventative replacement is dramatically cheaper than the £3,000–£5,500 replacement that follows a catastrophic failure.

cta.label: Compare 3.0 TDV6 engine prices for your X250 →

problems.title: 2.2 Diesel DPF & EGR Clogging
problems.rootCause: The 2.2‑litre diesel's DPF and EGR system clog with soot, particularly on urban‑use vehicles — leading to reduced power, increased fuel consumption, and eventual engine warning lights.
problems.recommendation: If your 2.2 diesel X250 is used primarily for short trips, a £150–£400 DPF and EGR service every 20k miles is cheap insurance against the £600–£1,200 replacement that follows.

problems.title: 5.0 V8 Water Pump & Cooling System Failure
problems.affectedModels: X250 5.0 V8 naturally aspirated and supercharged (2009–2015).
problems.rootCause: The 5.0 AJ133 V8's water pump is a known weak point — coolant leaks from the pump are common. Left unaddressed, overheating leads to head gasket failure or, in extreme cases, engine damage.
problems.recommendation: On any 5.0‑powered X250, a £250–£600 water pump replacement at the first sign of coolant loss is cheap insurance against the £4,000–£7,000 replacement that follows a neglected overheat.

cta.label: Compare 5.0 V8 engine prices for your X250 →

problems.title: 2.7 TDV6 Injector Failure
problems.rootCause: The 2.7‑litre V6 diesel's injectors can fail, causing rough idle, poor starting, black smoke, and increased fuel consumption. Injector failure is a well‑documented issue on early Ford‑PSA V6 diesels.
problems.recommendation: If your 2.7 TDV6 suffers from rough idle or poor starting, have the injectors inspected — a £200–£500 clean is far cheaper than the £800–£1,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare 2.7 TDV6 engine prices for your X250 →

problems.title: X250 Rear Subframe Corrosion (Non‑Engine)
problems.affectedModels: All X250 variants, particularly UK‑market cars.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but the most common non‑engine issue on the X250 — the rear subframe can corrode, particularly around the suspension mounting points, leading to MOT failure.
problems.recommendation: Have the rear subframe inspected on any X250 — it's the single most common reason an otherwise sound X250 fails an MOT or becomes uneconomical to repair.
